title: Seminar 1: Inside Innovation Systems for Sustainability Transitions

markdown.headingText: Seminar 1: Inside Innovation Systems for Sustainability Transitions
markdown.markdown:
The goal of this seminar is to provide you with a deeper understanding of why innovation systems are useful analytical tools. ## Learning Objectives {.unnumbered} ### Functions of Innovation Systems {.unnumbered} - Understand and describe how the motors of change [@hekkert2007FunctionsInnovationSystems] relate to the seven innovation system functions. ### Systems Thinking: Surprises of Innovation Systems {.unnumbered} - Realize bounded rationality of roles; actors can act reasonable to them but not to society - Delays in feedback loops make quick reactions difficult ### Sustainability {.unnumbered} - Why does sustainability need to be consistently negotiated by stakeholders? -How do innovation systems interact to affect sustainability? ## Simulation Game of Innovation and Sustainability {.unnumbered} 60 minutes of game play, 30 minutes of immediate reflection The overall aim of the game is to simulate how an innovation system navigates innovation for sustainability. Each role has their own personal goals in the game. They are stated in the summary table below. ### Description of Game Process {.unnumbered} The game has ten rounds with one round taking about five minutes. Overview of each round: 1. Innovation goals are set 2. Innovators draw cards 3. Innovators may play cards 4. Consumption 5. Discussion of outcomes and state of game 6. Negotiation of innovation goals There are two indicators that everyone in the game can see and influence. One representing the economy and one the environment. The environmental indicator ranges from 0 - 10; from completely destroyed to a perfectly intact environment. At the beginning of the game its value is 10. The economy indicator starts at 100 and develops from there. Each round the environmental indicator decreases by 100% (rounded to the nearest integer) of the economy indicator. > _Example:_ From the outcomes of the first round the economic indicator has grown to 150. Therefore the environmental indicator is decreased by 2 (1.5 rounded up to the nearest full number). Round 2 begins with the indicators at 150 and 8. Over the course of the game, you will: - fund innovation by deciding how many cards to draw from a deck; - innovate by playing matching cards; - consume as much as you want. The game ends after ten rounds, or if the environment is fully depleted. At the end of the game the special politician role faces an election. #### 1. Setting Innovation Goals {.unnumbered} At the beginning of each round, the innovation goals are formulated. While the university researcher is free to set their goals, the company researchers have to follow the orders of the industry player. > _Example:_ Industry decides that they want a new economic product innovation. If the innovators can, they must play a product innovation for the economy in step 3. At this step, the industry and the politician also decide how much they want to spend on innovation in this round. One card costs 5 points subtracted from the economic indicator. > _Example:_ The politician decides to spend 10 points. The university researcher gets to draw two cards. Industry decides to spend 20 points, it's innovators are allowed to draw 4 cards. #### 2. Innovators draw cards {.unnumbered} Each innovator draws cards according to their budget. They can decide if from which pile to draw: red stands for product and black for process innovations. #### 3. Innovation {.unnumbered} When the innovators have collected a pair of cards, they can play them as an innovation. The innovation then adds to the scoreboards. For an economic innovation, the sum of the face values is added to the economic indicator. If an environmental innovation is played, only half its sum is added, at the same time one point is added to the environmental indicator. If the innovators have both a process and a product at the same time, the effects of the innovation are doubled. > _Example:_ The innovators play the pair 6 of hearts and 6 of diamonds as an economic process innovation. The economic indicator increases by 12 points. The innovators play 4 kings as a radical environmental innovation; 20 points are added to the economic indicator (4*10/2) and the environment improves by 2. If no innovation can be played, the innovators keep the cards they drew in their hands. #### 4. Consumption {.unnumbered} Each round the consumers decide if they want to consume a regular product or an environmental product, adding 5 or 3 points to the economy indicator, respectively. The consumers aim maximize their consumption in accordance with their environmental preferences stated on their cards. #### 5. Evaluating the Outcomes {.unnumbered} The effects of all decision during the round are added to the indicators. #### 6. Discussion of Round and Negotiation of Next Round {.unnumbered} Each round ends with an open discussion of the events in the round. Were you satisfied? What should be done next round? ## Overview of Roles {.unnumbered} | Role Name | Goal | Special Task | Environmental Preference | |-------------------|---------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------| | Politician | Reelection | Fund University Researcher | | | Industry | Maximize Economic Indicator | Fund Company Researchers and Decide Their Innovation Type | | | Environmental NGO | Maximize Environmental Indicator | | | | Consumer | Maximize Consumption and Environmental Preference | Consume normally for 5 coins;
Consume sustainably for 3 coins. | Care {very much / some / not at all} for environment |
